speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was very interesting. I-- I used to think about how I got to Bergen-Belsen. And it's very interesting that we left the last camp, and we heard already the Russian troops coming from the east. And it was the last train that left for Bergen-Belsen. And if we would have just procrastinated instead of getting on this darn train, we would have been liberated by the Russian troops.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and sometimes I think about it, and I think to myself, you know, was my mother more anxious to go with the Germans than stay with the Russians, or didn't she quite comprehend the situation? Because, after all, she did come from the German culture, and she always used to say that in a land where the kind of music was composed and the kind of writers and-- and-- and poets lived, this kind of situation can never happen. And so I sometimes, when I reflect upon it, I think to myself, why didn't we stay behind? We knew. We heard people say the Russian front is approaching, and-- and-- and-- and the Russian troops were coming in in that direction, because that was already in-- in 19-- in 19-- '40-- '43-- '44-- '44, I think, when we left for Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very interesting, and-- and I sometimes think, you know, we are not very-- very smart as people. In fact, when I think about how we ever ended in the situation such as this, I had an aunt in Canada. My aunt in Canada came here before the war, and she wanted to bring us over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when she heard about the war, and I had some family in New York too, but why would anybody leave Poland when life was so good? We had everything. Why would we want to go to a country where we didn't even know the language? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, you know, when you think about it, people just never want to take up roots and take something new. There's always the fear that you might-- you might experience something worse than what you have. It's interesting. But anyway, my mom and I were on this train, this last train for Bergen-Belsen. And it was a terrible train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were on the train for quite a while. The train was shuffling back and forth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People defecated on the train. And the smell, and and the urine, and people crowded in pretty nearly standing position, and all. And when we got to Bergen-Belsen, there were an awful lot of sick people there-- an awful lot of sick people. And we were put into barracks. And I don't think my mother was the same at all. Not the same at all that she was before, because I think that that's when the sickness started. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sickness was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess it's a combination of several sicknesses. She died of typhoid and starvation and, for all I know, maybe even tuberculosis, because when the war ended, I had tuberculosis. So I wouldn't be surprised if she did as well. But it was definitely typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I sometimes think to myself, even so, she must have been a young woman, because she couldn't have been more than in her maybe late 30s, that she just gave up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to you before, I think some people were less afraid of death, and they were afraid of other things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think women were more afraid of being beat up and mutilated than being dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like for you in Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen, I wasn't quite as hidden. I had to go out for the selections. And the selections were at any time of the day and night, and people were called out to go outside. And we spent most of the winter in Bergen-Belsen. And there was no clothes and-- and no food, and there were an awful lot of corpses every morning. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It was not a camp where people worked. At least, I don't ever remember anybody going out to work. The-- the bunks, or the [INAUDIBLE], as they called them, were full of lice and full of bedbugs. And life was just absolutely horrific. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think, even as a child, I sometimes wonder if there was a will to live. I can't quite remember what a will to live is for a child. But I think that there was tremendous fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I think that I was there-- well, I was there until liberation. I was liberated by the Swedish Red Cross from Bergen-Belsen. So I must have been there at least six months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know if many people survived many more months than that in Bergen-Belsen. I also am not quite sure how long Bergen-Belsen as a camp really existed in the kind of situation that it did at the time. But I think that when one talks to people who were in Bergen-Belsen-- and I don't discuss it with people too much. There are more pleasant things for me to talk to people-- but when I do, then people are saying to me how horrible it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you mother die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died just a couple of weeks before liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was alone, maybe two, three weeks before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated by the Swedish Red Cross. I think it's Count Bernadotte who came into-- into the camp and liberated the children. There weren't that many children. I-- I have a picture, in fact, of some of the children. And I was not the youngest, but there weren't too many younger than myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation, I was taken to Sweden to Malmo. And we were put into a hostel-- more like a hospital hostel. And I-- I must have weighed-- maybe, I don't know-- maybe 50 pounds. And I had tuberculosis, and I had typhoid, and-- and my head was shaven, and so I was quite a sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this hostel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Sweden in several hostels. I was in Malmo for a little while for a few months, and I got a little better. And then they sent us to a hostel where there were children. And [INAUDIBLE] came from Israel, and the people from the Jewish agency in Sweden and around the world came and helped the children. And there we had the [INAUDIBLE] teach us and get us supposedly ready for some normality, whatever that was going to be. And some of these children eventually left for Israel. And so in Sweden, life was wonderful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed in at least four different hostels. I stayed in a place called Visingso, which is an island. I stayed in Billeshom. And then, towards the end, when most of the people left Sweden and just a few of us were left behind, because I could not immigrate to Canada-- and that's another story-- and I couldn't go to Israel because I still had tuberculosis, I lived as a little bit of a helper with a family in Stockholm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long before the tuberculosis cleared up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the tuberculosis did not clear up. But on a false x-ray, I came to Canada three years after the war, in 1948. I had an aunt-- I had an aunt here. This aunt is no longer with us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to live with her. And I was OK, but I did have some spots on my lungs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Would you believe it that I knew exactly the address and the name? And that was something that I was made to memorize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother, or your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was my father's sister, but I was made to memorize that name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you came to Canada in 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I contacted my aunt very soon after I was liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- the letter that went to my aunt was-- the-- the number of the house was very close, maybe two-- two-- now two digits.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're now a teenager, I think-- 16 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you share with us your impressions of when you first arrived in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I go back a little bit?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please, certainly.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Sweden, and I found my aunt, my mother's brother, who was also a survivor of the Holocaust, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started looking for the family. He looked for my mother. He looked for me. And I don't know if he looked for my brother or not, because I-- I don't really know if he thought that he was alive or not, but he looked for my mother and myself. And he found me in Sweden. And I imagine that in those days, everybody was finding-- making every effort to find, because there was the Swedish Red Cross, and the World Red Cross, and the [INAUDIBLE], and people were looking for people. So he found me. And he, at that time, was still in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was sick as well, but-- and I don't know which camps he was in, but he survived the war. And then, through the family, I also had some other family in New York, by the name of Biller. They were somewhat further removed. Cousins of second, third-removed cousins. And somehow they found out that I was there, maybe through my aunt here. And I started receiving parcels into Sweden with condensed milk and chocolate. And-- and-- and in fact, one of the cousins was a part-owner in the Hershey chocolates. So I had a lot of chocolates sent to me, and all this. So that's in-- in Sweden. And in Sweden, what was very interesting too, that we really and truly had a learning situation where the [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel and some of the people, some of the Jewish people, and-- and others, they made sure that we were being taught. It's in Sweden that I learned to read. It's in Sweden that I learned to write. It's in Sweden that I spoke Hebrew extremely well and learned the grammar and learned songs. And I think that the effort was to prepare us as halutzim to go to Israel. What happened, though, is that I was advised, because I was sick in that-- at that time, that to go to Israel for me was not a good idea. And that's where I wanted to go, because most of the kids went to Israel. They had no one, and they had no one in Canada, no one in the States, and-- and Israel, of course, was the haven. So most of them went to Israel, and I was terribly upset that I wasn't supposed to go to Israel. But my aunt was very anxious for me to come to Canada. And as I said, in 1948, I came to Canada on a false x-ray, which simply means that the x-ray that was produced for my exit was not my own, and it was a clear x-ray. That does not mean that I was contagious. I was not contagious, but I did have tuberculosis. And-- and-- and-- and for a while the sputum and the-- the-- the needle showed positive, et cetera. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I came to Canada, I came to my aunt, who had one daughter. And her name is Elizabeth. And surprisingly enough, we were named after the same person. You see, I was Elsa, and she was Elizabeth. And she is-- I always say to her she's older than me, but she's two weeks-- two weeks older than me. So I always take great pride that Elizabeth is older than me. And we went to school together. I went in to York Memorial Collegiate, the one on-- on Keele. And I registered, and that's a story in itself that's a very interesting one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went into the principal's office, and I said to him that I want to go to school. And of course, in those days, particularly in that area, because that's the west of Toronto, there weren't too many Jewish kids, and there weren't too many immigrant kids at the time. Today it's a different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- he said to me, well, how much education did you have? And I said, well, I was three years in Sweden. And in Sweden, I did some-- some work. I studied. And he asked me, would I-- he looked at my age, and he said to me, well, I don't really know what to do with you. We'll put you in grade nine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And grade nine was at least about two years younger than myself, the kids were. He put me in grade nine, and he said he'll take a chance. I think we developed a rapport as we were talking. And I don't know how we were talking, because my English was just a few words here and there. And again, in Sweden I did learn a little bit of English, where I could between the hands and-- and the expressions I could make myself a little understood. I was in grade nine for two weeks or three weeks. And I was put into grade 10, because they said that I will work hard enough to-- to succeed. And when I graduated from York Memorial, I graduated with first class honors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I finished grade-- grade 10, in the assembly hall, they said, we have here a student for the first time promoted to grade 11 [CRYING] with OK, OK, OK, OK in every subject. There were no marks. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then years later, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] many years later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very interesting, because the man who is-- who was director of the North York Board of Education saw me making a presentation to the board. And he said to me, you're Elsa. You sat a couple of rows behind me in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, who are you? And he said, I'm Carl Kinzinger. I said, Carl, you're the one that was the best student in German and in Latin before I came to York Memorial. But after I came, you weren't that anymore. [LAUGHS] And he had to laugh. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So it was very interesting, that-- that part. I don't know why I'm crying, because it's a-- it's a actually a pleasant memory. And the principal of York Memorial at that time, of course, he's many years gone. He was a man that was very highly respected.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in fact, when he died, there was a-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] a memorial service and a scholarship in his name. And he was called Papa Ainsley. And there were not too many people in those days that would have done that. I mean, in-- in-- in-- in school like Harvard Collegiate, where you saw a few more immigrants and all, it was more common. People were used to seeing Jewish students maybe coming in. But York Memorial was strictly Anglo-Saxon white. They-- they didn't know. I don't think they knew about the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was sort of interesting, and I have very fond memories of the school. There was one girl who was a Christian girl, very tall, very beautiful, and very blond, who took me under her wing and lived not too far from where I lived. And we walked to school every day together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in fact, now that I'm talking to you, I'm-- I send her a Christmas card every year, and she does the same to me. But it's time we got in touch. It's time to-- to touch base. Because for someone like me in that time, I needed someone, and here was someone who was kind and nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you had remarkable achievements scholastically. What about your social life? How was that shaping up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had lots of boyfriends. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about some of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [LAUGHS] Sure. Well, when I came-- when I came here, we didn't live in the Jewish area. My aunt lived on Old Weston Road, and I don't know if you know where Old Weston Road, but it's around Keele and that the area. My uncle had a-- a-- a dry goods store. And they were very hardworking people. They were very hard working people. They had two-- two stores that sold pants and shirts, and-- and nothing of-- of real substance that you would call today, but that's-- that was a working man's area. And my aunt stayed in one store, my uncle stood in the other store. And we-- we would go back and forth if she had to go upstairs to have a drink or make something to eat. The apartment was not very big. There were two bedrooms and a living room and a kitchen. And it was above a store. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] life was hard for my aunt and uncle. It wasn't an easy life that they had. They brought me over. I-- I know for a fact that a couple of members of the family felt, well, you know, a 16-year-old girl, you're bringing in a lot of tsuris. You are going to have a hard time. And-- and-- and they did. And I wasn't appreciative either. I was a pain. I was a real pain. I wanted to go to school. I wanted to go to university. I wanted to take music lessons. And they couldn't afford all that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They couldn't afford all that. But my cousin Elizabeth is just a wonderful individual, and I shared a room with her. And the room was very small, and I don't even remember if we had a closet. And she went to York Memorial too. She was the same age as myself, but she was a couple of-- one year ahead of me. She was a couple of years ahead of me to start with because of all-- all of this stuff. And she had a girlfriend, Adele, and Adele became my friend as well. And then I befriended a couple of people who were also Holocaust survivors. And so we used to go to what they called the [? Greenie ?] Club on Markham and to the Y on Brunswick. And I was quite popular. I had a lot of boys after me. And I liked boys. I always liked men. Don't make any bones about it. And I like the company of-- of people, and I had a good social life. I used to go to dances. I was busy many Saturdays. But I also studied very hard. I studied all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know my cousin sometimes wondered-- so much studying. So much studying. I had a lot to catch up. And even today, with all that I do and all the educational stuff that I do, I tell many people. I tell them that the gaps that I have-- the gaps that are missing-- I have never caught up with. I have been able to compensate, but I haven't caught up the gaps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and many times I think, you know, once I retire, which will be soon maybe, maybe I'll do something still to catch up some of the gaps. But I doubt it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he helped put on my boots one day on Brunswick someplace within the Y. I came-- I came with another fellow to the dance, but he was very nice, and he helped me. And we befriended each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was also one of the boys in the club, and I went out with a couple boys quite seriously before Howard. And I met Howard. And sometimes he says to me, in-- in all honesty, I think you got married because you wanted to get out of the house. How old were you when you got married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I married in 1951. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. It wasn't easy living with my aunt. It wasn't easy. It wasn't easy because I wasn't able to show any appreciation. I didn't have it in me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not sure I understand that. Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think that-- I think that-- I think that there was a tremendous hardness. I think there was a feeling I went through, and-- and-- and some things are coming to me. You are my aunt. I didn't love her. I had no relationship with her. She had this old-fashioned family feeling, and she-- she loved me in her way, and she wanted to help me. And she did for me everything. She was wonderful, but I didn't appreciate it. And I maybe didn't realize at the time, as I realized later, that they weren't able, financially, to do for me what I would have-- what I thought was coming to me, even. They just weren't able to do it. They lived in a-- in a little place. They worked hard. They didn't travel. They didn't do things for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here comes this young kid and wants this and that, and there's no piano in the house. What kind of house it is without an instrument? Without-- without-- and of course, Canada was without theaters and without-- without anything culturally sort of uplifting at the time. And-- and I was disappointed. I think we'll stop at this point. Thank you.
